# 1 Overview

## 1.1 Background

The Property & Casualty (P&C) insurance industry has long struggled withunderperforming portfolios where the root causes of poor profitability and high volatility remain unknown. Traditional approaches to data-driven decision-making in insurance have largely focused on dashboard reporting, which is limited by the general lack of advanced analytical capabilities which offers a more powerful alternative. 
By combining data science,  risk knowledge and industry expertise, insurers can generate actionable insights to grow, optimise existing portfolios and improve operational efficiency. As noted bythe Swiss Re Institute (2019), leveraging visual analytics can potentially help  investigate trends in underlying loss drivers, while data enrichment using external  data can help refine segmentation and underwriting strategy.

In this study, a motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset from a Spanish insurance company, made publicly available via Mendeley Data, is used to demonstrate the potential of visual analytics techniques in exploring and analysing key factors influencing portfolio profitability and volatility.

## 1.2 The Data

The data for this exercise is the motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset retrieved from [Mendeley Data](https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/sw4jmdb2sm/1).


The dataset contains **354,140 policy records** across **47 variables**, covering the period from 2022 to 2024. Variables are grouped into four broad categories:

| Category | Description |
|---|---|
| Policy & Insured characteristics | Policy type, status, business origin, payment frequency, bonus score |
| Driver & Vehicle characteristics | Driver age, vehicle age, fuel type, vehicle brand, vehicle value, area |
| Premiums | Total and coverage-level premiums collected |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | Claims counts, incurred costs, and exposure duration |

## 1.3 The Task

Using appropriate **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** methods and **ggplot2** functions, this study aims to:

- Apply suitable **univariate** visual analytics techniques to explore the distributions and characteristics of numerical and categorical variables.

- Apply suitable **bivariate** visual analytics techniques to investigate and statistically validate factors influencing the **profitability** and **volatility** of the insurance portfolio.

# 2 Loading Packages

| Package | Description |
|---|---|
| [**tidyverse**](https://www.tidyverse.org/) | A collection of R packages designed for data science, used for data import, wrangling and transformation |
| [**readxl**](https://readxl.tidyverse.org/) | For reading Excel files such as the variable cookbook |
| [**knitr**](https://yihui.org/knitr/) | For dynamic report generation and displaying formatted tables |
| [**patchwork**](https://patchwork.data-imaginist.com/) | For combining multiple ggplot2 plots into a single composite figure |
| [**ggthemes**](https://yutannihilation.github.io/allYourFigureAreBelongToUs/ggthemes/) | Extra themes, geoms and scales for ggplot2 |
| [**scales**](https://scales.r-lib.org/) | For formatting axis labels and annotations in ggplot2 |
| [**ggridges**](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/ggridges/vignettes/introduction.html) | For creating ridgeline plots to visualise distributions |
| [**ggdist**](https://mjskay.github.io/ggdist/) | For visualising distributions and uncertainty |
| [**ggpubr**](https://rpkgs.datanovia.com/ggpubr/) | For creating publication-ready ggplot2 plots |
| [**ggstatsplot**](https://indrajeetpatil.github.io/ggstatsplot/) | For combining statistical tests with visualisations |

The code chunk below uses `p_load()` of the [**pacman**](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/pacman/vignettes/Introduction_to_pacman.html) 
package to check if the packages are installed. If they are, they will be loaded into R. If not, they will be installed first before being loaded.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, readxl, knitr,
               patchwork, ggthemes, scales,
               ggridges, ggdist, ggpubr,
               ggstatsplot, naniar)
```
:::


# 3 Importing the Data

In this section, the following steps are taken to import and perform initial checks on the data, from reading in the raw data to verifying its structure. The **tidyverse** package will be used, specifically **readr** for reading in the data and **dplyr** for data manipulation.

The code chunk below imports the motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset using `read_delim()` of the **readr** package. The dataset uses a semicolon (`;`) as its delimiter instead of a comma, so `delim = ";"` is specified.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- read_delim("Dataset of motor insurance portfolio.csv",
                        delim = ";",
                        show_col_types = FALSE)
```
:::


The dataset is in a tibble dataframe, containing **354,140 observations (rows)** across **47 variables (columns)**. Each observation corresponds to a motor vehicle insurance policy record from a Spanish non-life insurance company.

We use `glimpse()` of the **dplyr** package to get a quick overview of the dataset structure, including the number of rows, columns and data types of each variable.

# 4 Data Wrangling

## 4.1 Variable Selection

Below are the variables retained for this study, which can be grouped into 4 broad categories of Policy & Insured Characteristics, Driver & Vehicle  Characteristics, Premiums, and Exposure, Claims & Incurred. Details are extracted from the cookbook(`Descriptive_of_variables.xlsx`):

| Category | Variable | Description | Type | Encoding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Policy & Insured | `policy_type` | Main coverage structure | CAT | TP, TPG, CC, COMP_E, COMP_N |
| Policy & Insured | `policy_status` | Policy active or cancelled | CAT | A - Active, C - Cancelled |
| Policy & Insured | `business_type` | Business origin | CAT | NB - New Business, P - Portfolio |
| Policy & Insured | `payment_frequency` | Billing frequency | CAT | A - Annual, S - Semi-annual, Q - Quarterly |
| Policy & Insured | `bonus_score` | Past claims experience | CAT | G - Good, N - Neutral, B - Bad |
| Driver & Vehicle | `driver_age` | Age of main driver | NUM | In years |
| Driver & Vehicle | `vehicle_age` | Age of insured vehicle | NUM | In years |
| Driver & Vehicle | `vehicle_value` | Declared insured value | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Driver & Vehicle | `fuel_type` | Vehicle fuel type | CAT | D - Diesel, G - Gasoline |
| Driver & Vehicle | `vehicle_brand` | Brand of vehicle | CAT | 65 brands |
| Driver & Vehicle | `municipality_type` | Geographic classification | CAT | I - Inland, C - Coastal, IS - Islands |
| Driver & Vehicle | `circulation_area` | Circulation environment | CAT | U - Urban, R - Rural |
| Premiums | `total_premium` | Total premium collected | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `liability_premium` | Liability coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `property_damage_premium` | Property damage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `theft_premium` | Theft coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `fire_premium` | Fire coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `glass_premium` | Glass coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `legal_protection_premium` | Legal protection premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Premiums | `occupants_premium` | Occupants coverage premium | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `total_exposure` | Time-on-risk in policy years | NUM | 0 to 1 |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `total_claims` | Total claims reported | NUM | Count |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `total_incurred` | Total incurred cost of claims | NUM | In euros (€) |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `loss_ratio`* | Profitability measure | NUM | total_incurred / total_premium |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `claim_frequency`* | Volatility measure | NUM | total_claims / total_exposure |
| Exposure, Claims & Incurred | `has_claim`* | Binary claim indicator | NUM | 1 if claims > 0, else 0 |

*Derived variables created in Section 4.5.

The code chunk below uses `select()` of the **dplyr** package to retain only the variables relevant to this study.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  select(insured_id, year,
         
         # Policy characteristics
         policy_type, policy_status, business_type,
         payment_frequency, bonus_score,
         
         # Driver & vehicle characteristics
         driver_age, vehicle_age, age_driving_licence,
         fuel_type, vehicle_value, seats,
         power_to_weight_ratio, vehicle_brand,
         municipality_type, circulation_area,
         
         # Premiums
         total_premium, liability_premium,
         property_damage_premium, theft_premium,
         fire_premium, glass_premium,
         legal_protection_premium, occupants_premium,
         
         # Exposure, claims & incurred
         total_exposure, liability_exposure,
         total_claims, total_incurred)
```
:::


The resulting dataset retains **354,140 observations** across **30 variables**.

## 4.3 Recoding Categorical Variables

`recode()` is primarily used to replace specific values in a vector with new ones. The recoded variable is then assigned using `mutate()` of the 
**dplyr** package.

::: panel-tabset
## Policy Type

`policy_type` is recoded to reflect the full coverage description:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_type = recode(policy_type,
    "TP"     = "Third-Party",
    "TPG"    = "Third-Party with Glass",
    "CC"     = "Third-Party Combined",
    "COMP_E" = "Comprehensive with Excess",
    "COMP_N" = "Comprehensive without Excess"))
```
:::


## Policy Status

`policy_status` is recoded to reflect whether the policy is active 
or cancelled:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_status = recode(policy_status,
    "A" = "Active",
    "C" = "Cancelled"))
```
:::


## Business Type

`business_type` is recoded to reflect the business origin:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(business_type = recode(business_type,
    "NB" = "New Business",
    "P"  = "Portfolio"))
```
:::


## Payment Frequency

`payment_frequency` is recoded to reflect the billing frequency:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(payment_frequency = recode(payment_frequency,
    "A" = "Annual",
    "S" = "Semi-Annual",
    "Q" = "Quarterly"))
```
:::


## Bonus Score

`bonus_score` is recoded to reflect the claims history:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(bonus_score = recode(bonus_score,
    "G" = "Good",
    "N" = "Neutral",
    "B" = "Bad"))
```

:::


## Fuel Type

`fuel_type` is recoded to reflect the vehicle fuel type:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}

    "D" = "Diesel",
```

## Municipality Type

`municipality_type` is recoded to reflect the geographic classification:

## 4.4 Data Type Conversion

From the `glimpse()` output earlier, the categorical variables are currently stored as character (`chr`) data type. They need to be converted to **factors** (`fct`) so that R treats them correctly in visualisations and statistical analyses. `mutate()` and `as.factor()` of the **dplyr** package are used to perform the conversion.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_type       = as.factor(policy_type),
         policy_status     = as.factor(policy_status),
         business_type     = as.factor(business_type),
         payment_frequency = as.factor(payment_frequency),
         bonus_score       = as.factor(bonus_score),
         fuel_type         = as.factor(fuel_type),
         vehicle_brand     = as.factor(vehicle_brand),
         municipality_type = as.factor(municipality_type),
         circulation_area  = as.factor(circulation_area),
         year              = as.factor(year),
         seats             = as.factor(seats))
```
:::

## 4.5 Variable Creation

Three new variables are created to support the analysis of portfolio profitability and volatility. `mutate()` of the **dplyr** package is used to create these variables.

### 4.5.1 Loss Ratio

`loss_ratio` is calculated as the ratio of total incurred costs to total premium collected. A loss ratio greater than 1 indicates that the insurer paid out more in claims than it collected in premiums, reflecting poor profitability.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(loss_ratio = total_incurred / total_premium)
```
:::


### 4.5.2 Claim Frequency

`claim_frequency` is calculated as the ratio of total claims to total exposure. It measures how often claims occur per policy year, reflecting the volatility of the portfolio.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(claim_frequency = total_claims / total_exposure)
```
:::


### 4.5.3 Has Claim

`has_claim` is a binary indicator variable that takes the value 1 if a policy has at least one claim, and 0 otherwise. It is useful for identifying which policies have experienced a loss.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(has_claim = ifelse(total_claims > 0, 1, 0))
```
:::

The *.rds* file format is usually smaller than its CSV counterpart and will therefore take up less storage space. The *.rds* file will also preserve data types and classes such as factors and numeric variables, eliminating the need to redefine data types after loading the file. For fast and space efficient data storage, the cleaned dataset is exported as an RDS file using `write_rds()` and can be re-imported using `read_rds()`.

```{.r .cell-code}
write_rds(insurance, "insurance_clean.rds")
```

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance <- read_rds("insurance_clean.rds")
```

# 5 Univariate Analysis

## 5.1 Target Variables

The target variables are `loss_ratio`, `claim_frequency`, `total_incurred` and `has_claim`, which measure portfolio profitability, volatility, claim severity and claim occurrence respectively. As some variables contain extreme outliers, the analysis is restricted to a reasonable range for visualisation purposes.Before diving into the Exploratory Data Analysis, the distributions of key variables are examined individually to better understand their characteristics and identify any notable patterns.

::: panel-tabset
## Loss Ratio

`loss_ratio` measures the ratio of total incurred costs to total premium collected. A loss ratio greater than 1 indicates that the insurer paid out more in claims than it collected in premiums.

The raincloud plot below combines a half-violin plot, boxplot and individual data points to provide a comprehensive view of the distribution of `loss_ratio`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance_lr <- insurance %>%
  filter(loss_ratio < 10,
         loss_ratio > 0)

ggplot(insurance_lr,
       aes(x = 0, y = loss_ratio)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill = "#4a7c6f") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2,
               outlier.shape = NA,
               fill = "#7aadab",
               alpha = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio",
       subtitle = "Policies with loss ratio between 0 and 10",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Loss Ratio") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank())
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The raincloud plot reveals that `loss_ratio` is highly right-skewed among policies with claims. Most policies record a loss ratio between 1.0 and 2.5, indicating that incurred costs typically exceed premiums collected. The long right tail suggests that a small number of policies experience exceptionally high losses, which significantly impacts overall portfolio profitability.


The histogram and boxplot below provide a complementary view of the distribution of `loss_ratio`.


::: {.cell}

## Has Claim

`has_claim` is a binary variable indicating whether a policy has recorded 
at least one claim (1) or no claims (0). A bar chart is used to visualise 
the proportion of policies with and without claims.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
insurance %>%
  mutate(has_claim = ifelse(has_claim == 1, 
                            "Has Claim", 
                            "No Claim")) %>%
  count(has_claim) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = has_claim,
             y = n,
             fill = has_claim)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5,
            size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Has Claim" = "#4a7c6f",
                                "No Claim" = "#7aadab")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Claim Occurrence",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Number of Policies") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-34-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The bar chart shows that **85.7%** of policies in the portfolio have not recorded any claims, while only **14.3%** have experienced at least one claim. This is typical of motor insurance portfolios where most policyholders do not make a claim in a given year. Despite being a small proportion, the 14.3% of policies with claims are of particular interest as they are the primary drivers of portfolio profitability and volatility.

# 6 Exploratory Data Analysis

In this section, bivariate visual analytics techniques are applied to investigate and statistically validate the factors influencing the profitability and volatility of the motor insurance portfolio. `ggbetweenstats()` of the **ggstatsplot** package is used to combine statistical tests with visualisations, providing both visual and statistical evidence of the relationships between variables.

## 6.1 Factors Affecting Loss Ratio (Profitability)

`loss_ratio` is used as the measure of portfolio profitability. Only policies with a loss ratio between 0 and 10 are included to exclude extreme outliers that may distort the analysis.

::: panel-tabset
## Bonus Score

The violin plot below examines whether `bonus_score` — reflecting a policyholder's past claims history — has a significant effect on `loss_ratio`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = bonus_score,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Bonus Score",
  xlab = "Bonus Score",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-47-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio across bonus score groups (p = 2.07e-19). Interestingly, policyholders with a **Good** bonus score have the highest median loss ratio of **2.02**, followed by **Neutral** (1.60) and **Bad** (1.44). This counterintuitive finding may be attributed to the fact that Good bonus score policyholders dominate the portfolio (n = 35,663) and tend to hold more comprehensive policies, which attract higher claim costs. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant, confirming that bonus score is a meaningful differentiator of portfolio profitability.

## Policy Type

The violin plot below examines whether `policy_type` has a significant effect on `loss_ratio`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = policy_type,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Policy Type",
  xlab = "Policy Type",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-48-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio across policy types (p = 5.09e-92). The **Third-Party** policy type has the highest median loss ratio of **4.29** (n = 241), which is notably higher than all other policy types. This may be due to the very small sample size of Third-Party policies, where a few large claims can heavily skew the median upward.

Among the remaining policy types, **Third-Party Combined** (2.25) and **Third-Party with Glass** (2.22) have higher median loss ratios compared to **Comprehensive with Excess** (1.74) and **Comprehensive without Excess** (1.78). This suggests that comprehensive policies, despite offering broader coverage, are associated with lower loss ratios possibly because comprehensive policyholders tend to be more careful drivers who pay higher premiums, resulting in better profitability for the insurer.

The Mann-Whitney test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio between fuel types (p = 3.48e-26). Gasoline vehicles have a higher median loss ratio of **2.22** (n = 10,902) compared to Diesel vehicles at **1.92** (n = 26,639). Although the effect size is small (r = -0.07), the difference is statistically significant, suggesting that gasoline vehicles tend to generate slightly less profitable policies than diesel vehicles. This could be attributed to differences in driving behaviour or vehicle characteristics between gasoline and diesel vehicle owners.

The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in claim frequency across policy types (p = 3.28e-298). **Comprehensive without Excess** policies have the highest median claim frequency of **2.00** (n = 4,836), followed by **Comprehensive with Excess** (1.40, n = 16,470). In contrast, Third-Party based policies have notably lower claim frequencies — **Third-Party Combined** (1.17, n = 24,157), **Third-Party with Glass** (1.18, n = 3,868) and **Third-Party** (1.22, n = 384). This suggests that comprehensive policyholders tend to make claims more frequently, possibly because broader coverage lowers the threshold for filing a claim. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant.

## 6.3 Distribution Comparison across Key Groups

To complement the statistical analysis in Sections 6.1 and 6.2, ridgeline plots are used to compare the full distribution of `loss_ratio` and `claim_frequency` across key grouping variables. Ridgeline plots are particularly useful for visualising how distributions shift across multiple groups simultaneously.

::: panel-tabset

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true"}
  ggplot(aes(x = loss_ratio,
             y = policy_type,
             fill = policy_type)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7,
                      scale = 0.9,
                      quantile_lines = TRUE,
                      quantiles = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Comprehensive with Excess"    = "#4a7c6f",
    "Comprehensive without Excess" = "#7aadab",
    "Third-Party"                  = "#d4e8e1",
    "Third-Party Combined"         = "#2d5a51",
    "Third-Party with Glass"       = "#a8d5c2")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio by Policy Type",
       subtitle = "Vertical line shows median",
       x = "Loss Ratio",
       y = "Policy Type") +
  theme_minimal() +

```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Take_Home_Ex01_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-57-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The ridgeline plot provides a clearer view of how loss ratio distributions differ across policy types. **Comprehensive with Excess** and **Third-Party Combined** show the narrowest and most concentrated distributions, peaking sharply around 1–2, indicating consistent and predictable loss behaviour. **Comprehensive without Excess** and **Third-Party with Glass** display 
slightly wider distributions with longer right tails, suggesting greater variability. Most notably, **Third-Party** policies exhibit a distinctive bimodal distribution with two peaks, one around 1–2 and another around 4–5, suggesting two distinct subgroups with very different loss behaviours. This may be attributed to its small sample size (n = 241) where a few extreme cases disproportionately influence the distribution shape.

Overall, the ridgeline plot reinforces that Comprehensive policies generate more stable and predictable loss ratios, while Third-Party policies, particularly basic Third-Party exhibit greater variability and higher losses.

# 7 Summary

This study applied univariate and bivariate visual analytics techniques to explore a real-world motor vehicle insurance portfolio from a Spanish non-life insurance company, with the aim of uncovering key factors influencing portfolio profitability and volatility.

## Key Findings

**Portfolio Overview**

The portfolio is dominated by middle-aged drivers holding Third-Party Combined policies, with the vast majority of policyholders recording no claims. This suggests a generally low-risk customer base, with a small but significant minority of high-risk policyholders driving most of the portfolio's losses.

**Profitability (Loss Ratio)**

Policy type emerges as the strongest differentiator of portfolio profitability. Basic Third-Party policies are the least profitable despite being the least common, while Comprehensive with Excess policies generate the most consistent and stable loss ratios. This suggests that broader coverage does not necessarily translate to higher losses, in fact, comprehensive policyholders tend to be more careful drivers who pay higher premiums.

Fuel type and circulation area, while statistically significant, have negligible practical impact on profitability and are unlikely to be meaningful underwriting factors on their own.

**Volatility (Claim Frequency)**

Bonus score is the most powerful predictor of claim frequency. Policyholders with a Bad bonus score reflects a history of past claims, make significantly more frequent claims, contributing disproportionately to portfolio volatility. This confirms that past claims history is a reliable indicator of future claim behaviour.

Comprehensive without Excess policies stand out as the most volatile policy type, likely because the absence of an excess removes the financial disincentive to file small claims. In contrast, Third-Party Combined policies show the most stable and predictable claim behaviour.

## Recommendations for Future Research

- **Confirmatory Data Analysis** — Future studies should incorporate additional statistical tests such as regression modelling to validate the findings from this exploratory analysis and establish more definitive relationships between the identified risk factors and portfolio outcomes.

- **Exploring Additional Variables** — A more exhaustive analysis incorporating a wider range of variables could yield deeper insights into portfolio profitability and volatility. Variables such as driver occupation, annual mileage, claims history duration and vehicle usage type could provide a more complete picture of risk segmentation.

- **Longitudinal Analysis** — Conducting longitudinal studies to track changes in loss ratios and claim frequencies over a longer time period can reveal trends and seasonal patterns that a three-year snapshot may not capture.

- **Incorporating External Data** — As highlighted by the Swiss Re Institute (2019), enriching the portfolio data with external sources such as traffic density, weather patterns and regional accident statistics could further refine segmentation and improve underwriting strategy.
